issue_title: 404 : Not Found Error on Notebook start
issue_body:
I'm trying to setup a basic Jupyterhub server that spawns notebooks locally using the systemdspawner.  I've gone through the installation of both according to the instructions, and my Jupyterhub server starts as expected.  But immediately after logging in I get an error:
404 : Not Found
You are requesting a page that does not exist!

I can't see anything relevant or problematic in the logs, but this is the first time I've played around with Jupyter so I'm probably missing something obvious...

I've attached a cleaned version of the jupyterhub_config.py file, the runtime log for the jupyterhub server (with debug options on), and the syslog from the time I tried to access the notebook through the server.


[404error-syslog.txt](https://github.com/jupyterhub/systemdspawner/files/1300020/404error-syslog.txt)
[hub-log-404error.txt](https://github.com/jupyterhub/systemdspawner/files/1300019/hub-log-404error.txt)

[jupyterhub_config-trimmed.txt](https://github.com/jupyterhub/systemdspawner/files/1299997/jupyterhub_config-trimmed.txt)

issue_title: NTAG I2C is reported as NTAG 203
issue_body: An NTAG I2C 1K is discovered and reported as NTAG 203. Easily reproducible by running examples/tagtool.py.

issue_title: Need help with LLCP!!!
issue_body:
I am trying to make two Raspberry Pi talk though llpc.
At one end:
import nfc
import nfc.llcp
import threading

def server(socket):
    message, address = socket.recvfrom()
    socket.sendto("It's me!", address)
    socket.close()

def on_startup(llc):
    socket = nfc.llcp.Socket(llc, nfc.llcp.LOGICAL_DATA_LINK)
    socket.bind(address=32)
    threading.Thread(target=server, args=(socket,)).start()
    return llc

llcp_options = {
    'on-startup': on_startup,
    #'on-connect': on_connect,
}
with nfc.ContactlessFrontend('tty:AMA0:pn532') as clf:
    clf.connect(llcp=llcp_options)
    print("link terminated")


The other end:
import nfc
import nfc.llcp
import threading

def client(socket):
    socket.sendto("Hi there!", address=32)
    socket.close()

def on_connect(llc):
    socket = nfc.llcp.Socket(llc, nfc.llcp.LOGICAL_DATA_LINK)
    threading.Thread(target=client, args=(socket,)).start()
    return True

llcp_options = {
#     'on-startup': on_startup,
    'on-connect': on_connect,
}
with nfc.ContactlessFrontend('tty:AMA0:pn532') as clf:
    clf.connect(llcp=llcp_options)
    print("link terminated")


This may work for the first time after reboot, but after that:
link terminated
Exception in thread Thread-1:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/threading.py", line 810, in __bootstrap_inner
    self.run()
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/threading.py", line 763, in run
    self.__target(*self.__args, **self.__kwargs)
  File "caonima.py", line 6, in server
    message, address = socket.recvfrom()
  File "build/bdist.linux-armv7l/egg/nfc/llcp/socket.py", line 141, in recvfrom
    return self.llc.recvfrom(self._tco)
  File "build/bdist.linux-armv7l/egg/nfc/llcp/llc.py", line 819, in recvfrom
    return socket.recvfrom()
  File "build/bdist.linux-armv7l/egg/nfc/llcp/tco.py", line 296, in recvfrom
    raise err.Error(errno.EPIPE)
Error: nfc.llcp.Error: [EPIPE] Broken pipe

Really need some help here!

issue_title: Export to HTML does not save Google Charts.
issue_body:
While exporting an IPython notebook to HTML, the google charts are missing in the generated HTML. We have noticed this with - treemap and sankey diagrams.

issue_title: Use new obfuscation mode to improve performance
issue_body:
* Encrypt
  - Compile source to code objects, or load .pyc as code objects
  - Iterate code object, co_code of each code object will be wrapped by an armor as the following format
```
    0 JUMP_ABSOLUTE            n = 3 + len(bytecode)

         ...                                   here it's obfuscation bytecode by des or some other algorithms

n + 3:
    0 LOAD_GLOBAL              ? (armor)
    3 CALL_FUNCTION            0
    6 POP_TOP
    7 JUMP_ABSOLUTE            0
```
* Decrypt
  - For each encrypted code object, first op is jump to n + 3
  - At n + 3, it will call a PyCFunction to decrypt those obfuscation bytecode
  - When it returns, jump to first op of really bytecode 

By this way, no import hooker, no setprofile/settrace required.  The performance will be improved remarkably.

issue_title: Outputs '# SKIP' on a test that is actually run
issue_body:
In pytest, a 'strict' xfail test is one that is expected to fail, and when it passes, that is considered to be a test failure.
I have the following test

```
import pytest
@pytest.mark.xfail(strict=True)
def test_fail():
    # This errors, which means the test passes
    assert 1 == 2
```

which reports:

```
ok 1 - test_fail # SKIP 
1..1
```

The 'ok' part is correct, but the 'SKIP' is incorrect, since it did in fact run the test. (This can confuse the user, especially when obfuscated by a TAP driver)

Interestingly, the test 

```
import pytest
@pytest.mark.xfail(strict=True)
def test_fail():
    # This test case doesn't error, which means the test should fail
    assert 1 == 1
```

reports:

```
not ok 1 - test_fail
# [XPASS(strict)]
1..1
```

Which is correct and doesn't have the above problem. However, when 'strict=False', it still fails, which isn't correct (the strict flag seems to be ignored in all cases).

issue_title: Updates on the tasks branch.
issue_body:
1. Unify handling of tasks. Previous, the default task execution method is a plain fork/process. It has been now changed to save task, execute by `sos execute`, monitor by `sos status`.
    * Pro: Unified approach makes sure a locally executable task would be executable remotely. A few tasks in tests have been fixed.
    * Pro: tasks could now be monitored externally.
    * Pro: windows compatibility. The method did not work under windows because different ways processes are started under windows.
    * Cons: MUCH slower because of all the overhead. Multiple processes, commands are involved, and there are complex status monitoring procedures. This makes tasks unsuitable for large number of small (a few seconds) tasks.

2. Unify local and remote execution. The new `Host` class uses `_host_agent` to handle local and remote host.

3. Framework for different tasks. The new `Host` class now has a `_task_engine` object that should interactive with different task engines.

PENDING:

1. Truly independent task. Right now tasks would be killed when sos exists. 
2. `sos run -w`: The no-wait interface. This involves the implementation of external task manager.
3. `sos kill` ...

issue_title: Rerun of completed tasks with missing output
issue_body:
A seeming simple scenario: Run a script with task, task completed. Remove output files, re-run, and sos will find that task already completed. In this case,

1. `sos run -s default` will still check signature and reaches the `task` statement because of signature mismatch.
2. underlying `sos execute task -s default` will refuse to run a completed task.
3. `sos run -s force` will rerun the step and task, but innocent steps/tasks will also be rerun.

This is a new problem with the new task format because previously sos has a `signature-mismatch` status for tasks. That is to say, when we check if a task is completed, it will check if the signature matches, so `sos execute task -s default` will execute if the signature mismatch.

Now, before we restore this `signature-mismatch` status, we need to think what this status means.

This status essentially binds the `task` file with its outputs, so a task would be considered failed whenever the input or output files are moved or removed. Considering you have a large number of jobs on the cluster, it is almost inevitable that input output files will be moved around after jobs are completed. 

There are also technical difficulties in getting this status. Because input and output files are stored outside of task files. We will have to read a task file, get its signature, and check input output files, potentially even calculate md5 of all these files. Considering doing this for ten thousand jobs that you have done in the past month each time you check the status of jobs? We can of course cache the status but we still need to check a lot of files to at least see if their timestamp has been change between each `sos status` check.

Now related to #1015 if we are re-executing a step with lots of jobs, jobs with now removed output files will be rerun, which is not acceptable in many cases.

So we are somehow stuck here with #1015...

```python
import numpy as np
import ray
import time

M = 100
N = 100
P = 100

ray.init()

@ray.remote
def load_data(i):
  return np.random.normal(size=10000)

@ray.remote
def aggregate(x, y):
  return x + y

@ray.remote
def simple_function(x):
  time.sleep(np.random.uniform(0.1, 0.2))
  return x

data = [load_data.remote(i) for i in range(M)]
while len(data) > 1:
  data.append(aggregate.remote(data.pop(0), data.pop(0)))
results = ray.get(data)

previous = simple_function
cumulative = 0
for _ in range(N):
  result_ids = [simple_function.remote(cumulative) for _ in range(P)]
  cumulative = sum(ray.get(result_ids))  
```

Script for making json (run this in the same interpreter).

```python
import json

tasks = ray.global_state.task_table()
objects = ray.global_state.object_table()
clients = ray.global_state.client_table()

local_scheduler_ids = []
for k, v in clients.items():
  for client in v:
    if client["ClientType"] == "local_scheduler":
      local_scheduler_ids.append(client["DBClientID"])

def random_worker_id():
  return ray.utils.binary_to_hex(np.random.bytes(20))
fake_worker_ids = dict()
for local_scheduler_id in local_scheduler_ids:
  fake_worker_ids[local_scheduler_id] = [random_worker_id() for _ in range(10)]

json_tasks = []
for task_id, task_info in tasks.items():
  try:
    task_info["WorkerID"] = np.random.choice(fake_worker_ids[task_info["LocalSchedulerID"]])
  except:
    print("Handling driver task.")
    task_info["WorkerID"] = 20 * "ee"
  task_info["TaskSpec"]["ReturnObjectIDs"] = [ray.utils.binary_to_hex(obj_id.id()) for obj_id in task_info["TaskSpec"]["ReturnObjectIDs"]]
  args = task_info["TaskSpec"]["Args"]
  new_args = []
  for arg in args:
    if isinstance(arg, ray.local_scheduler.ObjectID):
      new_args.append(("ObjectID", ray.utils.binary_to_hex(arg.id())))
    else:
      new_args.append(("Value", arg))
  task_info["TaskSpec"]["Args"] = new_args
  json_tasks.append(task_info)

tasks_dump = json.dumps(json_tasks)

json_objects = []
for obj_id, obj_info in objects.items():
  new_obj_info = dict()
  new_obj_info["ObjectID"] = ray.utils.binary_to_hex(obj_id.id())
  new_obj_info["Manager_IDs"] = obj_info["ManagerIDs"]
  new_obj_info["IsPut"] = obj_info["IsPut"]
  new_obj_info["TaskID"] = obj_info["TaskID"]
  json_objects.append(new_obj_info)

objects_dump = json.dumps(json_objects)

parent_edges = []
for task_info in json_tasks:
  parent_edges.append((task_info["TaskSpec"]["ParentTaskID"], task_info["TaskSpec"]["TaskID"]))

parent_edges_dump = json.dumps(parent_edges)

task_object_edges = []
object_task_edges = []
for task_info in json_tasks:
  task_id = task_info["TaskSpec"]["TaskID"]
  for return_id in task_info["TaskSpec"]["ReturnObjectIDs"]:
    task_object_edges.append((task_id, return_id))
  for arg in task_info["TaskSpec"]["Args"]:
    if arg[0] == "ObjectID":
      object_task_edges.append((arg[1], task_id))

task_object_edges_dump = json.dumps(task_object_edges)
object_task_edges_dump = json.dumps(object_task_edges)

with open("json_task_vertices.json", "w") as f:
  f.write(tasks_dump)

with open("json_object_vertices.json", "w") as f:
  f.write(objects_dump)

with open("json_parent_edges.json", "w") as f:
  f.write(parent_edges_dump)

with open("json_task_object_edges.json", "w") as f:
  f.write(task_object_edges_dump)

with open("json_object_task_edges.json", "w") as f:
  f.write(object_task_edges_dump)
```

The schema produced by the above is as follows.

1. `json_task_vertices.json` This consists of all the "task vertices". It is a list of task vertex objects. A task vertex has the following schema.

    ```python
    # Task Vertex:
    #   - LocalSchedulerID  # The ID of the local scheduler that scheduled this task (equivalent to the machine that the task ran on).
    #   - State                       # One of "DONE", "RUNNING", "SCHEDULED", "WAITING", "QUEUED", "LOST", "RECONSTRUCTING"
    #   - WorkerID                # The ID of the worker process that executed the task.
    #   - TaskSpec               # More information about the task
    #       - TaskID                # The ID of the task.
    #       - ActorID               # The ID of the actor that this task ran on. If this task was not run on an actor, then this will be 0xffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffffff.
    #       - DriverID              # The ID of the "driver" process that created this task.
    #       - FunctionID          # the ID of the function that is executed in this task (We should probably also add the function name into this schema).
    #       - ParentTaskID      # The ID of the task that spawned this task. One of the tasks is the "driver process.
    #       - ReturnObjectIDs # A list of the IDs of the return values of this task.
    #       - Args                     # A list of the arguments to this function (a combination of simple python objects and object IDs).
    ```

2. `json_object_vertices.json` This consists of all the "object vertices". It is a list of object vertex objects. An object vertex has the following schema.

    ```python
    # Object Vertex:
    #   - ObjectID      # The ID of this object.
    #   - IsPut             # True if the object was created by a call to "ray.put" and false otherwise.
    #   - TaskID          # The ID of the task that created this object.
    #   - ManagerIDs  # The IDs of the "object managers" that have a copy of this object.
    ```

3. `json_parent_edges.json` This is a list of "parent edges". A parent edge is a tuple of two task IDs, and the first one is the ID of the task that spawned the second task.

4. `json_task_object_edges.json` This is a list of data dependencies. Each element is a pair of IDs. The first element of the pair is a task ID that produced the object ID which is the second element in the pair.

5. `json_object_task_edges.json` This is a list of data dependencies. Each element is a pair of IDs. The first element of the pair is an object ID that was passed as an argument to the task whose ID is the second element in the pair.

NOTE:
- There are probably some edges that I failed to capture.
- I tried to simplify things by providing the edges in separate json objects. However, these objects are redundant and all information there is already captured in the task vertices and the object vertices.

TODO:
- I probably need to generate task-object dependencies by looping over the objects instead of over the tasks because I think looping over the tasks will miss objects created by put (alternatively maybe I should loop over the objects after to pick out the ones created by put).
- I should probably leave the task vertices and object vertices as dictionaries instead of lists.

cc @yifanwu

issue_title: API for specifying resource requirements at task submision time
issue_body:
(Initial discussion in #1236.)

It would be useful to be able to specify resource requirements when submitting a task (especially RAM -- for example, the same task running a ML task being applied to a grid of learners and datasets, varying in size; in this case we can specify how much the task is going to consume before launching it, but this information is not static).

The current infrastructure is in place to support specifying resource requirements at tasks submission time, however it can only be done now via `ray.remote()` decorator which attaches them to tasks statically; the current workaround is doing something like this, creating a new wrapper for each execution:

```python
def f(x):
    pass

ray.remote(resources={"Memory": 1})(f)(3)
ray.remote(resources={"Memory": 2})(f)(4)
```

A cleaner API would be nice; maybe something like this (or whichever else if anyone has better ideas):

```
@ray.remote
def f(x):
    pass

f.with_resources(Memory=1).remote(3)
f.with_resources(Memory=2).remote(4)
```

ec2_resource1=boto3.resource('ec2', 'us-east-1')

issue_title: Dataset add should not attempt to auto-match lineage datasets if they exist in db
issue_body:
When adding datasets to the database `datacube dataset add` does roughly following

- For every input dataset
   1. Auto-match dataset to a product (fail if not found)
   2. For every dataset in the lineage of an input dataset
         1. Auto-match dataset to the product (fail if not found)

If you are indexing derived dataset with lineage that already exists in the database, then second auto-match is not necessary, those datasets are already in the database, and are already associated to a product. 

This behaviour makes `--dtype` option behave in a way that most users won't expect, when indexing datasets with lineage. Here is a help string for this option:

```
  -t, --dtype TEXT                Product to be associated with the datasets
```

Several problems already

1. Using `dtype` name that is highly associated with `numpy.dtype` in the target community, but is not *that kind of dtype*
2. Actually means `dataset-type` == `product`
3. Inconsistent naming on one line: *"dataset type"* on a command line, *"product"* in the help string
4. You can actually specify multiple products, help doesn't make this clear

What this option really is: 

> Restrain auto-matching to a subset of products from the database (you can supply several on a command line), auto-matching is applied to the new datasets listed on the command line and any lineage datasets referenced in the metadata.

### User experience scenario

1. Created some new datasets using stats tool or similar (so they have lineage data)
2. Added product definition for my new datasets
3. Tried running with default options and was told that my datasets could match to several products in the DB and so no indexing can proceed
   - `datacube product add` will not warn you when adding products with "too similar" metadata
4. No worries I can specify product with this weirdly named `--dtype` option
5. Try running `datacube dataset add -t my_new_product my_new_dataset.nc`, still get an error

But I just told you which product to use!!?

The problem is that now that you have restrained set of products to match against to just one product you care about, you can't auto-match any of the lineage products at all. 

But you should not need to, those datasets are already in the DB, no need to auto-match them in this case. Sure you still need to auto-match IF and ONLY IF lineage datasets are not in the DB already.

### Conclusion

Combination of poor documentation and decision to auto-match before checking DB makes `--dtype` option unusable to anyone but those who are ultimately familiar with the implementation of `datacube dataset add` command.

The following code fragment emits log lines to STDERR with the default format style even though no logging is requested. I suspect that `ipyparallel` is doing something dirty.

    from parsl import DataFlowKernel
    from parsl.configs.local import localIPP as config

    dfk = DataFlowKernel(config=config)
    dfk.cleanup()

The above code with the minor change of using threads will not emit the log lines.


    from parsl import DataFlowKernel
    from parsl.configs.local import localThreads as config

    dfk = DataFlowKernel(config=config)
    dfk.cleanup()


Please help test by running this with the latest parsl code.

issue_title: Kubernetes python client unable to refresh access token
issue_body:
I'm running a script that calls into kubernetes via the python client. However, it appears unable to get the correct credentials. I /can/ use the `kubectl` command-line interface, which I've noticed populates my `.kube/config` file with an access-token and an expiry whenever I make a command (e.g., `kubectl get pods`). As long as that token has not expired, my python script runs fine. However, once that token expires it doesn't seem to be able to refresh it, instead failing and telling me to set `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS`. 

Of course, when I created a service-account with a keyfile and pointed `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS` to that keyfile, it gave me the following error:

```
RefreshError: ('invalid_scope: Empty or missing scope not allowed.', u'{\n  "error" : "invalid_scope",\n  "error_description" : "Empty or missing scope not allowed."\n}')
```

Although from the code it seems like you _can_ provide scopes to credentials, I am trying to create a Kubernetes client via `kubernetes.config.kube_config.new_client_from_config`, which seems to create credentials without scopes.

Is there something wrong with my understanding of this client? Appreciate any help with this!


I am using the 3.0.0 release of the kubernetes python library. In case it is helpful, here is my `.kube/config`: 
```
apiVersion: v1
clusters:
- cluster:
    certificate-authority-data: <CERTIFICATE_DATA>
    server: <IP_ADDRESS>
  name: <cluster_name>
contexts:

users:
- name: <cluster_name>
```

issue_title: Line的叠加问题
issue_body:
@chfw 各位大神，在本人的操作中，需要遇到和issue #197类似的问题，不清楚pyecharts中有无相同功能。首先上代码

```
from pyecharts import  Line
line = Line("外温与pack最大温度的关系")
areas = ["beijing", "qingdao","ningbo"]
for area in areas:
    weather_avg_temp = TemperatureData.loc[TemperatureData["area"] == area].groupby(["weather_temp"],as_index = False)["max_temp"].mean()
    v1 = weather_avg_temp["weather_temp"]
    v2 = weather_avg_temp["max_temp"]
    line.add(area, v1, v2,is_stack = True)
line
```
目的想要画北京、青岛、宁波的一个参数与外温的曲线关系三个图画出来如下
![](https://i.imgur.com/4ylDC6G.png)

而如果单独画一个城市的曲线（如北京的）

      
![](https://i.imgur.com/802U42I.png)
可以看出温度的最低从-10开始，而三条线那个图则是从1开始。
看代码的表述是在多条线在一个坐标轴上的时候，用index来代替真实值。
我想问的是，有没有办法让线像画散点图那样分布在直角坐标系中，而不是通过这种index做替换。

issue_title: Need to support work without STDOUT and/or STDERR
issue_body:
We can figure with non-stop or useless output from command, which can eat all our memory.
Example: `cat /dev/zero` will eat memory and block us in few seconds.

Solution: allow to not pull `STDOUT` and/or `STDERR` and just work with timeouts and exit code.
In addition: set default timeout as real time instead of unlimited.

issue_title: Bug: dead lock once connection session timeout?
issue_body:
hi, 

I found the thread will never be notified once it can not owned the lock and wait on the syncPoint.await() (line 125) and then the connection session timeout and reconnect again.  The the lock will be dead since the lock file on zookeeper server already been deleted and it will never be notified again. 

How to reproduce:
    1. write client code to make it wait on a lock (some other thread write a lock file on server first)
    2. disconnect the network by dropping the line
    3. reconnect it after a long enough time (after the server thinks client session timeout and delete the lock file on server)
Then you will find the lock file on zookeeper server already gone and the reconnected client will wait on the SyncPoint forever.

Reason:
    the session timeout event zookeeper client library send to DistributedLockImpl was droped wrongly by the line 266:
                        if (!event.getPath().equals(watchedNode)) {
	267 	LOG.log(Level.INFO, "Ignoring call for node:" + watchedNode);
	268 	return;
	269 	}

How to fix:
    you may want to move the line 266 test to the else if (event.type = Event.EventType.NodeDeleted) block. 

Could you take a look and fix this bug?

By the way, I am curious are this code is still using by twitter?

Thanks 
billmuch

issue_title: reorganize namespaces/python packages/directories for consistency
issue_body:
We should have the same scoped path to various bits of code in Python, C++, and in the on-disk layout of our headers.

I propose the following (note that I don't care much about the actual names, just the consistency):
```
galsim/artifacts/....
galsim/distort/...
...
galsim/sbprofile/_sbprofile.so
galsim/meas/hsm/_hsm.so
...
include/galsim/sbprofile/<sbprofile headers>
include/galsim/meas/hsm/<hsm headers>
...
src/sbprofile/<sbprofile source>
src/meas/hsm/<hsm source>
pysrc/sbprofile/<sbprofile wrapper code>
pysrc/meas/hsm/<hsm wrapper code>
```

In addition, the SBProfile C++ code should be in namespace ```galsim::sbprofile```, and the HSM code should be in namespace ```galsim::meas::hsm```.  Of course, right now, HSM doesn't have any C++ headers, as it's just a C executable, but that would naturally be added if we convert it to a library with a Python interface.

issue_title: feature: Graph.preferredLabel() (skos:prefLabel before rdfs:label)
issue_body:
joernhees, 2011-08-26T11:21:41.000Z 
Hi,

I needed an easy way to get labels for a subject, preferring skos:prefLabels and filtered by language from an rdflib graph.
Currently there only is a Graph.label(subject) which will give you any existing rdfs:label, which won't return skos:prefLabel triples and can't filter for language tags.

I implemented Graph.preferredLabel(subject,lang,labelProperties). labelProperties (defaults to (skos:prefLabel, rdfs:label)) you can specify the preference and filter for lang-tag if wanted.


code can be found here:
http://code.google.com/r/joernhees-rdflib/source/list
rev: d5d2203b07cf to b982c0fc9443
